confidence.disclosure: Muhammad Dara Shikoh, the eldest son of Shah Jahan and the heir apparent of the Mughal Empire, is well documented in the Padshahnama of Lahori, the chronicles of the war of succession (Khafi Khan, Bernier, Manucci), and through his own works - the Majma' al-Bahrayn, the translations of the Upanishads (Sirr-i Akbar), and a substantial divan. The major events of his life - birth, marriage to Nadira Banu, his sieges of Kandahar, the defeat at Samugarh, his flight, capture, and execution - are firm by year and many by day.

sources: Francois Bernier, Travels in the Mogul Empire
Supriya Gandhi, The Emperor Who Never Was: Dara Shukoh in Mughal India (2020)

journey.event: Born on 20 March 1615 in Ajmer in Rajasthan, where his father Prince Khurram (the future Shah Jahan) was then encamped. He is the eldest surviving son of Khurram and his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal. His birth was attributed to the blessings of the Sufi saint Mu'in al-Din Chishti, whose shrine in Ajmer he would venerate throughout his life.

On 17 June 1631 his beloved mother Mumtaz Mahal dies at Burhanpur giving birth to her fourteenth child. Dara is sixteen. The death devastates Shah Jahan, who builds the Taj Mahal in her memory; it also leaves Dara emotionally even closer to his father.

On 1 February 1633 marries his cousin Nadira Banu Begum in a magnificent ceremony at Agra. The marriage, arranged by his mother before her death, is unusually happy; Dara never takes another wife (extraordinary for a Mughal prince) and remains devoted to Nadira until the end of her life.

Around 1640 meets and becomes a disciple of the Qadiri Sufi master Mulla Shah Badakhshi, who has settled in Lahore. Through Mulla Shah Dara enters deeply into the Sufi path. From this point on, mysticism and the search for spiritual unity become the dominant concerns of his intellectual life.
journey.source: Gandhi, The Emperor Who Never Was

journey.event: By 1642 his father Shah Jahan has formally designated Dara as the heir apparent, granting him the title Shah Buland Iqbal and reserving him at the imperial court rather than sending him to the provinces. Dara is given a high mansab and continues to grow in his father's favour while his three brothers gain experience as governors in distant provinces.

Composes his first major spiritual work, the Safinat al-Awliya (Vessel of the Saints), a collection of biographies of Sufi masters in Persian. He follows it with the Sakinat al-Awliya (Tranquillity of the Saints), focused on his own master Mulla Shah, and other works.

Leads a Mughal expedition in 1653 to retake Kandahar, which had been seized by the Safavids in 1649. The siege fails after several months and he returns unsuccessful. This is the third unsuccessful Mughal siege of Kandahar in five years (after those of his brothers Aurangzeb and Murad Bakhsh). The failure damages his military reputation just as the succession question is starting to loom.

In 1655 completes his most famous work, the Majma' al-Bahrayn (The Confluence of the Two Oceans), a Persian treatise comparing the technical vocabulary of Sufism and the Hindu Vedanta and arguing that the inner truths of the two traditions are essentially the same. It is one of the boldest works of comparative religion produced anywhere in the early modern world.

In 1657 completes his crowning work, the Sirr-i Akbar (The Great Secret), a Persian translation of fifty Upanishads done with the help of a team of Sanskrit pandits brought from Benares to Delhi. Argues in his preface that the Upanishads are the 'hidden book' (kitab al-maknun) referred to in the Quran (56:78). The Sirr-i Akbar will later be translated into Latin by Anquetil-Duperron in 1801 and through this route the Upanishads will reach European thought.

In September 1657 his father Shah Jahan falls seriously ill in Delhi. As the heir apparent, Dara takes over the administration. But his three brothers - Shah Shuja in Bengal, Aurangzeb in the Deccan, Murad Bakhsh in Gujarat - all immediately rebel, refusing to recognise Dara's claim. The war of succession begins.

On 29 May 1658, at the Battle of Samugarh near Agra, Dara's army (despite being larger and better equipped) is decisively defeated by the combined forces of Aurangzeb and Murad Bakhsh. Dara fights bravely from the back of an elephant but at a critical moment dismounts to mount a horse, his troops think he has been killed, and the army disintegrates.

Flees to Delhi, then to Lahore, then to Multan, with Aurangzeb's forces in pursuit. Tries to raise new armies but is repeatedly defeated. The Mughal nobility, watching the war from a distance, are unwilling to commit to the losing side.

Driven into Sind, then into Gujarat, then back to Sind, then towards the Bolan Pass and Iranian territory. In June 1659 his beloved wife Nadira Banu, exhausted and ill, dies somewhere in the wastes of Sind. Dara, broken, sends her body with an escort to be buried in Lahore, beside his Sufi master.

In June 1659, while fleeing through Baluchistan towards Persian territory, Dara is betrayed by his host Malik Jiwan, a chieftain whose life Dara had once spared, and handed over to Aurangzeb's troops. He is brought back to Delhi as a prisoner.

Paraded in chains on the back of a filthy elephant through the streets of Delhi, where the population - which had loved him - watches in shocked silence. A council of religious scholars (assembled by Aurangzeb) declares him an apostate from Islam on the basis of his syncretic writings. On the night of 30 August 1659 (or 9 September by some accounts) his head is severed in his prison cell and brought to Aurangzeb to confirm the deed. His body is buried in an unmarked grave in the Humayun's tomb complex in Delhi.
